content.text: Total AI spend is a useful control metric, but it does not tell engineering or product teams whether the money is well spent. A team that spends $50,000 on tokens may be serving ten million customer conversations efficiently or wasting money on a broken agent loop that retries thousands of failed tasks. Unit economics metrics — cost per conversation, cost per task, and cost per active user — connect the spend number to the volume of work delivered, transforming a finance metric into an engineering signal.

attrs.title: Three unit metrics derived from total AI spend

content.text: The three core unit metrics

Each metric answers a different question and is computed at a different granularity. The three metrics form a nested hierarchy: cost per task aggregates to cost per conversation, which aggregates to cost per active user when normalised by the user base.
Metric
Formula
Primary use
Who consumes it
Cost per conversation
Total token cost of all turns in one conversation / conversation count
Is the conversation-level cost sustainable vs. revenue per conversation?
Product management, finance
Cost per task
Token cost of one agent step / task count
Which specific agent actions are expensive and should be optimised?
Engineering, platform teams
Cost per active user (CPAU)
Monthly total AI spend / monthly active users
Is AI cost scaling proportionally with the user base?
Finance, executive team
Defining 'conversation' in an agentic context

In a simple chat interface, a conversation is a sequence of user and assistant turns sharing a context window. In an agentic system the definition is more complex: a single user instruction may trigger multiple model calls through a pipeline of specialist agents, tool-call loops, and retrieval steps. The organisation must define a conversation boundary before computing the metric.
Session-scoped conversation: all model calls triggered between a user session start and end event. Suitable for interactive copilots with session tracking.
Request-scoped conversation: all model calls triggered by a single user request, including recursive agent calls. Suitable for request-response APIs with a stable request ID.
Workflow-scoped conversation: all model calls in a named workflow run (e.g., one 'document review' or 'code generation' invocation). Suitable for batch and automation workloads.
Choose the boundary that most closely maps to the business transaction for which value is delivered — this makes the unit cost comparable to revenue per transaction.
Instrumenting cost per conversation

Computing cost per conversation requires linking every model API call to the conversation it belongs to. The standard approach is to pass a conversation ID in request metadata and aggregate token usage by that ID in the cost ledger.
Computing cost per task at sub-conversation granularity

Cost per task measures the token cost of a specific agent action type — a tool call, a retrieval step, a generation, or a classification. It is the most granular unit metric and the most useful for engineering optimisation because it identifies which specific operations are expensive.

The p95 cost per task is more actionable than the average: a task type with an average cost of $0.01 but a p95 of $0.50 has occasional outlier calls consuming 50× the expected budget. These outliers usually indicate runaway loops, unexpectedly long contexts, or error-recovery paths that should be short-circuited.
Cost per active user (CPAU)

CPAU translates AI spend into a per-user metric that finance and product teams can benchmark against revenue per user (RPU) and target margins. It is the most executive-friendly unit metric because it directly answers 'how much does it cost us per user to deliver AI features?'
Setting benchmarks and targets for unit metrics
Unit metrics are useful only relative to a target or benchmark. Without a target, the team has no way to know whether the current cost per conversation is good, poor, or improving.
Internal benchmarks
Establish a baseline during the first month of production operation; document the workload characteristics (model, average turn count, average input length) alongside the metric.

Track month-over-month trend: a rising cost per conversation is a red flag even if absolute spend is within budget — it indicates that the conversation is getting more expensive over time, possibly due to prompt growth or context window expansion.
Decompose changes: when cost per conversation rises, determine whether it is driven by longer conversations (more turns), larger prompts (more tokens per turn), a model upgrade, or a new agent hop.
External benchmarks
Workload type
Typical cost per conversation
Source
Customer support chatbot (simple FAQ)
Industry reports 2024–2025
Document analysis copilot (multi-step)
Enterprise deployments
Code generation (multi-file)
Developer tool benchmarks
Autonomous research agent (multi-hop)
Agentic framework reports
External benchmarks for agentic AI are still maturing. Treat them as rough orientation rather than precise targets, and supplement with internal competitive intelligence where available.
Using unit metrics to guide optimisation priorities

Unit metrics make optimisation decisions quantifiable. Rather than debating whether to invest in prompt compression or model migration, compute the expected impact of each option on cost per conversation and rank them by ROI.
If cost per conversation is rising due to context window growth: investigate whether long conversation histories can be summarised or truncated after N turns without quality loss.
If cost per task is high for retrieval steps: evaluate whether vector search can replace a full model call for initial filtering, reducing expensive generation to only the final synthesis step.

If CPAU is above target despite reasonable cost per conversation: the problem is likely conversation frequency (users making too many requests) — implement rate limiting or investigate whether users are repeating requests due to poor quality responses.

If cost per conversation varies 10× across user segments: segment the metric by use case and investigate high-cost outliers separately — they likely represent problematic edge cases, not representative usage.
Cohort analysis: unit metrics across user segments

Aggregate unit metrics hide important variation across user segments, use cases, and feature cohorts. A single cost per conversation number for a customer support tool may average together a low-cost FAQ cohort ($0.01/conversation) and a high-cost escalation cohort ($0.80/conversation). Splitting the metric by cohort reveals whether the high-cost users are generating proportionally higher value.
Segment by use case: separate the metric for distinct workflow types (FAQ, escalation, document review) and track each independently.
Segment by user tier: enterprise users may engage in longer, more complex conversations than free-tier users — compare the metric against the revenue each tier generates.
Segment by channel: web, mobile, and API consumers often have different conversation patterns and cost profiles.
Segment by agent version: when deploying a new agent version, track unit costs for the new version vs. the old version in parallel before full cutover to confirm cost neutrality or improvement.
Unit cost trend monitoring and alerting

A unit metric that was healthy last month can degrade silently if prompt lengths grow, agent pipelines gain hops, or a new model tier is introduced. Continuous monitoring with automated alerts catches degradation before it becomes a budget problem.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List
import statistics

@dataclass
class UnitCostMonitor:
    metric_name: str
    history: List[float] = field(default_factory=list)  # rolling 12-month values
    alert_threshold_pct: float = 15.0  # alert if current > trailing avg by this %

    def add_observation(self, value: float) -> dict:
        self.history.append(value)
        if len(self.history) < 3:
            return {"status": "insufficient_history"}

        trailing_avg = statistics.mean(self.history[-4:-1])  # 3-month trailing average
        pct_change = (value - trailing_avg) / trailing_avg * 100

        result = {
            "metric": self.metric_name,
            "current": round(value, 6),
            "trailing_avg_3mo": round(trailing_avg, 6),
            "pct_change": round(pct_change, 1),
            "alert": abs(pct_change) > self.alert_threshold_pct,
        }
        return result

# Example
monitor = UnitCostMonitor("cost_per_conversation_usd")
history = [0.055, 0.058, 0.061, 0.060, 0.059, 0.063, 0.072]  # last 7 months
for v in history:
    result = monitor.add_observation(v)

print(result)
# pct_change will be ~16.4% → alert=True, signalling the latest month is anomalous
Reporting unit metrics to product and engineering teams

Unit metrics are most valuable when they are visible to the teams whose decisions drive them — not just to FinOps. A monthly unit economics report shared with engineering leads and product managers creates accountability and surfaces optimisation opportunities earlier than a finance-only budget review.
Report section
Audience
Cadence
Cost per conversation by workflow type
Product managers
Monthly
Cost per task by agent action
Engineering leads
Monthly
CPAU trend vs. RPU target
Finance + executive
Monthly
Top 10 conversations by cost (anonymised)
Engineering
Weekly
p95 vs. average cost per task type
Platform engineering
Weekly

The 'top 10 conversations by cost' report is particularly valuable — it surfaces real production edge cases that engineers can investigate. A $12 conversation in a system where the average is $0.08 is almost always a diagnosable bug or a runaway loop, not a legitimate high-value interaction.
Common mistakes
Measuring total spend without normalisation: total spend grows with volume regardless of efficiency; unit metrics reveal whether cost per unit is improving or worsening as scale increases.

Defining 'conversation' inconsistently across teams: when team A counts agent sub-calls as separate conversations and team B rolls them up, the metrics are not comparable — standardise the definition before comparing.
Not tracking p95 cost per task: high average costs with wide variance indicate outlier calls that warrant investigation; the average alone masks this.
Setting CPAU targets without connecting them to revenue per user: a $5 CPAU is great if RPU is $50 and problematic if RPU is $8.
Treating all task types as equivalent: a classification call at $0.001 and a 10,000-token generation at $0.30 should be tracked separately, not averaged together.
Key takeaways
Three unit metrics matter: cost per conversation (product sustainability), cost per task (engineering efficiency), and CPAU (executive financial health signal).
Instrument every model API call with a conversation ID and task type before computing unit metrics — the raw data must be structured to support aggregation.
Track p95 cost per task alongside the average — outliers drive disproportionate spend and are usually fixable engineering problems.
Connect CPAU to revenue per user to determine whether the AI feature is financially sustainable at the current cost structure.
Use unit metrics to rank and quantify optimisation opportunities: the metric that is most above target with the largest volume is the first priority.
